summary: Gruber delivers a scathing self-critical analysis of Apple's handling of its 'more personalized Siri' features, arguing that Apple promoted vaporware at WWDC 2024 and doubled down by advertising non-existent features in iPhone 16 commercials. He establishes a four-level hierarchy of product 'realness' — from live demos to shipping products — and notes that the personalized Siri features were never demonstrated at any level, making them effectively vaporware. Gruber argues this represents a fundamental breach of the credibility Apple painstakingly built over 25 years, comparing it to the concept-video era of pre-Jobs Apple that nearly went bankrupt. He concludes that decision-makers within Apple knowingly promoted features they couldn't deliver, squandering the company's most valuable intangible asset, and calls for a Steve Jobs-style accountability reckoning from Tim Cook.
key_points: Apple's personalized Siri features were never demonstrated at any level — not even by Apple employees on controlled devices — making them literal vaporware promoted through concept videos

Gruber establishes a four-tier hierarchy of product realness (company demo → press hands-on → beta → shipping) and notes all shipped Apple Intelligence features were at level 1 at WWDC, while personalized Siri was at level 0
Apple compounded the problem by advertising these non-functional features in an iPhone 16 TV commercial, which has since been quietly pulled from YouTube

The core fiasco isn't being late on AI — it's that Apple told a story they knew wasn't true, squandering decades of accumulated credibility that distinguished post-NeXT Apple from the Sculley/Spindler/Amelio era
Gruber draws a direct parallel to Jobs's MobileMe accountability moment and argues Cook needs to hold a similar reckoning or risk letting mediocrity take root
money_quotes: Concept videos are bullshit, and a sign of a company in disarray, if not crisis.
You can stretch the truth and maintain credibility, but you can't maintain credibility with bullshit.
Damaged is arguably too passive. It was squandered. This didn't happen to Apple. Decision makers within the company did it.
The fiasco is that Apple pitched a story that wasn't true, one that some people within the company surely understood wasn't true, and they set a course based on that.

They announced something Apple couldn't do. And now they look so out of their depth, so in over their heads, that not only are they years behind the state-of-the-art in AI, but they don't even know what they can ship or when.
When mediocrity, excuses, and bullshit take root, they take over. A culture of excellence, accountability, and integrity cannot abide the acceptance of any of those things.
Promoting what you hope to be able to someday ship is way easier and more exciting than promoting what you know is actually ready to ship.
key_insight: Apple's promotion of personalized Siri features it could never even demonstrate — then advertising them in iPhone 16 commercials — represents a fundamental breach of the credibility the company spent 25 years building, echoing the concept-video dysfunction of its near-bankruptcy era.
